content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

NOVEMBER 21, 1991


Burton W. Oliver
Executive Director
State Board of Equalization
1020 N Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-259a

Dear Mr. Oliver:

     You have requested advice on behalf of the members of the
State Board of Equalization ("the Board") regarding the
application of the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1  This letter provides clarification of advice
previously provided to Earl J. Cantos, Jr., as a representative of
Earnest J. Dronenburg, Jr., a member of the Board. (Cantos Advice
Letter No. I-91-259.)

QUESTIONS

     1.  If no more than 200 copies of the annual report are
mailed in a single calendar month, does Regulation 18901 apply?

     2.  May each member independently distribute 200 copies or
less of the same item each month without violating Regulation
18901?

CONCLUSIONS

     1.  If no more than 200 copies of the annual report are
mailed in a single calendar month, Regulation 18901 does not
apply.  Therefore, the annual report could include a picture of
each member, a biography, and a letter signed by the current
chairman of the Board.


_________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 507 • Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-259a
Page 2


     2.  Each member may independently distribute 200 copies or
less of the same item each month without violating Regulation
18901.

                              FACTS

     Prior to the passage of Proposition 73, the annual report of
the State Board of Equalization included pictures of each member,
a biography, and a letter signed by the current chairman.  With
the passage of Proposition 73, these items were deleted from the
annual report pending clarification of the new prohibitions on
mass mailings as public expense.  The Board is now preparing the
annual report for 1990-90 which will include pictures, a signed
letter from the chairman, and biographies of the individual
members of the Board.

     Most copies of the annual report will be distributed within
the agency, to officers of other agencies, or pursuant to
unsolicited requests from members of the public.  Approximately
370 copies will be provided to the individual members for
independent distribution.

                             ANALYSIS

     The Act provides that a newsletter or other mass mailing2 may
not be sent at public expense.  (Section 89001.)  The Commission
has interpreted this provision of the Act to apply only to mass
mailings which include the names, photographs, signatures or other
references to elected officers.  (Regulation 18901.)

     Regulation 18901 provides that a mailing is prohibited only
if all of the following apply:

          (1)  Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to
the recipient at his or her residence, place of
employment, or post office box.  For
purpose of this subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered
to the recipient must be a tangible item, such as a
videotape, record, or button, or a written document.

          (2)  The item sent either:

               (A)  Features an elected officer affiliated
with the agency which produces or sends the
mailing, or


_________________________
2  A mass mailing is defined as "over two hundred substantially
similar pieces of mail, but does not include a form letter or
other mail which is sent in response to an unsolicited request or
other inquiry."  (Section 82041.5.)

Our File No. A-91-259a
Page 3


                (B)  Includes the name, office, photograph, or
          other reference to an elected officer affiliated
          with the agency which produces or sends the
          mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation,
          consultation, coordination, or concert with the
          elected officer.

                (3)(A)  Any of the costs of distribution is paid
          for with public moneys; or

                (B)  Costs of design, production, and printing
          exceeding $50.00 are paid with public moneys, and
          the design, production, or printing is done with
          the intent of sending the item other than as
          permitted by this regulation.

                (4)  More than two hundred substantially similar
          items are sent, in a single calendar month, excluding
          any item sent in response to an unsolicited request and
          any item described in subdivision (b).

                                    Regulation 18901(a).

     Thus, if 200 or less copies of an item are sent in a calendar
month, the mass mailing prohibitions do not apply.  As we
previously advised, such a mailing may include the name,
photograph, signature or other reference to an elected officer
without violating the mass mailing prohibitions of the Act.
(Cantos Advice Letter, supra, page 4.)

     Therefore, if only 200 or less copies of the Board's Annual
Report are distributed in a calendar month, the report may contain
the members photographs and biographies and may include a letter
signed by the current chairman.

     In tallying the number of copies distributed, copies which
are otherwise exempted under subdivision (b) of Regulation 18901
are not counted.  This would include, for example, annual reports
sent to Board employees, officers, deputies and other staff in the
normal course of business from the Board (18901(b)(4)); annual
reports sent in the normal course of business to the Board to another
governmental entity or officer (18901(b)(3)); and annual reports
sent to members of the public pursuant to unsolicited requests
(18901(a)(4)).

     Finally, where elected state officers, such as members of the
Board (Sections 82021 and 82024) send items independently, items
sent by one member will not be cumulated with items sent by
another member.  Thus, each member may independently distribute

Our File No. A-91-259a
Page 4


200 or less copies of the annual report each month without
violating Regulation 18901.3

     I trust that this clarifies our advice with respect to
distribution of your annual report consistent with the mass
mailing provisions of the Act. If you have any further questions
or require any further clarification, you may contact me at (916)
322-5901.4

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin,
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By:  Margaret W. Ellison
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SH:MWE




_________________________

3  We would again advise you, however, that if a coordinated
mailing were sent by members of the Board in an effort to
circumvent the mass mailing provisions, the total number of items
sent would be cumulated for purposes of determining whether the
mailing is a "mass mailing."

4  Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
